content.text: A class decorator is a function applied to a class declaration using the

syntax. At runtime the decorator receives the class constructor as its only argument and can read or modify it — adding methods, wrapping the constructor, recording metadata, or registering the class with a framework. Angular relies heavily on class decorators:

attrs.alt: Class decorator execution flow: class defined, decorator factory called, decorator function receives the class, return value optionally replaces the class

content: Class decorators run once at class declaration time (module load), not at instantiation.
They receive the class constructor — not the instance. Modifying prototype methods affects all future instances.

content.text: with TC39 stage-3 decorators (the modern spec) have subtle differences. Angular currently uses legacy decorators.

content: Returning a new class from a decorator loses TypeScript's inference of added members (requires explicit type casting or declaration merging).
Angular decorators are not designed to stack with your own custom class decorators on the same class — they may conflict over prototype mutation.

content.text: A class decorator can register security metadata that a route guard reads at runtime:
Use a class decorator to mix in capabilities from multiple source objects into the target class:
TypeScript 5.0+ added support for the ECMAScript stage-3 decorator proposal. The signature differs from the legacy
syntax. Angular 19+ uses this spec by default for new applications.

Angular 17+ uses a build-time compiler that transforms @Component, @Injectable, etc. — you rarely need to write raw decorators yourself. Understanding the mechanism helps when debugging DI issues or reading Angular source code.
Testing Code That Uses Class Decorators
are processed by the Angular compiler — you test decorated classes through TestBed, not by calling the decorator function directly.

content: A class decorator is a function called with the class constructor at class declaration time.
Decorator factories wrap the decorator in a function that accepts configuration options.
Decorators can read/add methods, replace the constructor, or record metadata via reflect-metadata.
Multiple decorators on one class are evaluated bottom-up; their returned functions run top-down.
